skill.name: azure-compliance
skill.description: Guides Azure compliance audits with Azure Quick Review, Resource Graph queries, Key Vault expiration checks, and remediation guidance for common cloud security findings.
skill.summary: Audit Azure compliance, Key Vault expiration, and resource configuration risks.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported a critical combination of command execution, network access, credential access, weak cryptography, and sensitive key patterns. Human review found these are mainly markdown command examples, Azure documentation links, SDK snippets, and Key Vault metadata workflows, with no evidence of prompt injection or hidden executable code. The skill has medium operational risk because it can guide Azure inventory scans and remediation commands that affect cloud resources.

medium_findings.title: Azure Inventory and Key Vault Metadata Access
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs assistants to list subscriptions, resource groups, keys, secrets, and certificates for compliance review. This is legitimate for an Azure audit skill, but it can expose sensitive cloud inventory and Key Vault metadata if used without proper authorization.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe Azure MCP tools for subscription, resource group, Key Vault key, secret, and certificate inspection. The context confirms legitimate audit intent, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Remediation Examples Can Change Azure Resources
medium_findings.description: The remediation reference includes Azure CLI and PowerShell examples that create private endpoints, update storage and Key Vault settings, enable backup, and change public network access. These examples are not auto-executed by the skill, but they can materially alter cloud configuration if a user runs them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are concrete resource-changing examples in markdown code fences. There is no evidence they execute automatically, but the operational impact is clear if copied into an Azure environment.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Detections Are Markdown Examples
low_findings.description: The many Ruby backtick, shell substitution, and PowerShell detections occur inside markdown tables or fenced command examples. They do not show executable skill code, dynamic evaluation, or command injection logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are markdown snippets and identifiers, not runtime code. This strongly supports a false-positive verdict for direct code execution.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Detections Are Not Implementations
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits appear in recommendation text, column labels, Key Vault guide text, or SDK examples rather than insecure cryptographic implementation. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, or signature code that uses a weak algorithm for real processing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive documentation or SDK usage notes. I did not find evidence of executable weak cryptographic logic in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Certificate and Key File Detections Are SDK References
low_findings.description: Sensitive certificate or key file detections map to SDK import and client builder examples, not embedded private keys, certificates, or credential files. The examples use Azure identity credentials and placeholder vault URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines contain language imports and client construction using placeholders. There are no literal private keys, certificates, or secret values in these locations.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Azure Endpoints
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL findings are Azure documentation links or placeholder Key Vault endpoint formats. They do not indicate data exfiltration endpoints, tracking beacons, or unexpected network destinations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are recognizable Microsoft documentation links or placeholder Azure vault domains. No suspicious third-party collection endpoint was found.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cloud Resource Enumeration Workflow
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill supports Azure subscription, resource group, Resource Graph, and Key Vault listing workflows. This is expected for compliance auditing but should be used only with authorized Azure tenants and least-privilege reader access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The files explicitly describe enumeration tools and query tables. The behavior is intentional for the skill, but it is a meaningful security-sensitive pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Resource-Changing Remediation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes examples that update Azure security settings and networking controls. These are remediation templates, not automatic execution, but they require human review before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present and can change live Azure resources if executed. The markdown context reduces the risk because the skill itself does not run them directly.

content.value_statement: Azure teams need fast evidence about compliance gaps, expired Key Vault items, and risky resource settings. This skill guides structured scans, triage, and remediation planning with Azure Quick Review and related references.
actual_capabilities: Guides Azure Quick Review scans for subscriptions, resource groups, management groups, and service-focused assessments.
Explains how to analyze azqr workbook sheets, recommendation categories, and impacted resource lists.
Audits Key Vault keys, secrets, and certificates for expired, expiring, disabled, or missing expiration states.
Provides Azure Resource Graph query patterns for tag, network exposure, role assignment, and lock checks.
Maps common compliance findings to Azure CLI, Bicep, and PowerShell remediation examples.
Summarizes findings by severity, impact, scope, and recommended next action.
limitations: Requires authenticated Azure access and enough permissions to read the selected scope.
Does not independently verify live Azure state unless connected tools or user-provided scan output are available.
Remediation examples need review before execution because they can change production resources.
Does not replace formal compliance certification, legal review, or organization-specific control mapping.

use_cases.title: Quarterly Subscription Compliance Review
use_cases.description: Cloud security teams run azqr, review high-impact findings, and create a prioritized remediation plan.

use_cases.title: Key Vault Expiration Monitoring
use_cases.description: Azure administrators identify expired or soon-expiring keys, secrets, and certificates before services fail.

use_cases.title: Governance Query Development
use_cases.description: Platform teams build Resource Graph queries to check tags, public access, role assignments, and resource locks.

prompt_templates.title: Run a Basic Compliance Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-compliance skill to plan an Azure Quick Review scan for my subscription. Ask for any missing scope details first.

prompt_templates.title: Check Key Vault Expiration Risk
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-compliance skill to audit Key Vault items for expired, expiring, disabled, or missing expiration values. Summarize by severity.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze azqr Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-compliance skill to review these azqr findings. Group them by security, reliability, cost, and operations impact.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Remediation Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-compliance skill to create a phased remediation plan for these Azure compliance findings. Include risk, effort, owner, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Review this Azure Quick Review result summary for a production subscription.
output_examples.output: A severity-ranked compliance summary with critical security exposures first, followed by reliability gaps, governance issues, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Find Key Vault items that may break services soon.
output_examples.output: A Key Vault expiration report listing expired items, items expiring within the threshold, resources without expiration dates, and rotation recommendations.

output_examples.input: Help me query resources missing required tags and public access controls.
output_examples.output: A set of Resource Graph query patterns with plain-language explanations and guidance for scoping results safely.

best_practices: Run scans with least-privilege reader access before requesting any remediation permissions.
Validate every remediation command in a non-production scope before applying it to production.
Track findings over time and rerun scans after changes to confirm remediation worked.
anti_patterns: Do not run remediation commands against production without change approval and rollback planning.
Do not treat azqr findings as final compliance certification without control owner review.
Do not expose Key Vault names, secret names, or subscription details in public issue trackers.

faq.question: What Azure permissions does this skill need?
faq.answer: Most audit workflows need Reader access. Key Vault checks may also need data plane permissions to list metadata.

faq.question: Can it remediate findings automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides remediation examples and planning guidance. Users should approve and run changes through normal operations controls.

faq.question: Does it inspect secret values?
faq.answer: The documented workflow focuses on metadata such as expiration and enabled state. It should not require reading secret values.

faq.question: Can it analyze an existing azqr workbook?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide the relevant scan output or summaries, and it can group findings and recommend next actions.

faq.question: Is it useful without Azure MCP tools?
faq.answer: Yes. It can guide Azure CLI and Resource Graph workflows, but live verification depends on available tools and permissions.

faq.question: Which AI tools are supported?
faq.answer: The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
